passages.explanation: A month has passed, and Dorian is now installed in Lord Henry's world of luxurious aestheticism -- satinwood tables, Persian rugs, gold-topped bottles. His reading of Manon Lescaut, a novel about a beautiful woman who destroys a young man through his devotion to her, is a deliberate foreshadowing. Dorian has already absorbed Henry's habits (he is impatient, restless, expects to be entertained), showing how thoroughly influence has reshaped him even in small behavioral ways.

The brief appearance of Lady Henry is devastatingly funny and deeply sad. She is the living consequence of Lord Henry's philosophy applied to marriage: a nervous, scattered woman whose husband treats her with elegant contempt. Her observation that Wagner's music is 'so loud that one can talk the whole time' is comic but also captures the essential loneliness of her marriage. She is the wreckage that aestheticism leaves in its wake, a person reduced to a comic sketch by someone who values wit over kindness.

Lord Henry's aphorisms about marriage and women are simultaneously brilliant and repellent, and Wilde intends both responses. Dorian's announcement that he is in love marks a crucial development: he is trying to live out Henry's philosophy of new sensations, but love is precisely the kind of genuine feeling that Henry's worldview cannot accommodate. Henry's dismissal of women as 'a decorative sex' and his clinical 'analysis' of them reveals the deep misogyny embedded in his brand of aestheticism -- beauty is to be consumed, not respected.

Dorian's account of finding Sibyl Vane is structured as a quest narrative inspired directly by Lord Henry's teachings -- he went 'in search of some adventure,' seeking 'new sensations.' The antisemitic caricature of the theatre manager reflects ugly period attitudes that Wilde reproduces without critique. More importantly, Dorian's rapturous description of Sibyl reveals that he loves her not as a person but as an actress: she is 'every age and every costume,' a vehicle for art rather than a human being. He loves the roles, not the woman, which prefigures the catastrophe to come.

Lord Henry's response to Dorian's love story is characteristically double-edged: he encourages and undermines simultaneously. His claim that 'the people who love only once in their lives are really the shallow people' redefines faithfulness as a failure of imagination, continuing his systematic inversion of conventional morality. The distinction between loving a person and loving what they represent is sharpened when the theatre manager's bankruptcies 'due to the Bard' are called 'an honour' -- in this worldview, ruin in pursuit of beauty is noble, while prudence is contemptible.

Dorian's account of meeting Sibyl deepens the troubling pattern: she calls him 'Prince Charming,' replacing his real identity with a fairy-tale archetype, just as he replaces her identity with her stage roles. Neither sees the other as a real person. Dorian's declaration that Sibyl is 'sacred' provokes Henry's most revealing response: 'It is only the sacred things that are worth touching.' This captures the aesthete's relationship to innocence -- it exists to be violated, because the violation itself creates a new sensation. Dorian's plan to 'get her out of the Jew's hands' and launch her in the West End treats Sibyl as a project, not a partner.

Lord Henry's satisfaction at watching Dorian's transformation is explicitly compared to scientific observation, and his admission that Dorian is 'to a large extent his own creation' reveals the experiment's true nature: Henry is not just observing life but directing it. Dorian's casual cruelty toward Basil -- calling him 'a bit of a Philistine' -- shows how completely Henry's values have displaced Basil's. The portrait arriving in its beautiful frame while Dorian drifts away from its creator dramatizes the split between art and artist that Wilde explored in the Preface.

Lord Henry's private meditations are the novel's most philosophically dense passages and also its most morally troubling. His view of Dorian as a 'subject made to his hand' for psychological experimentation, his pleasure in being the cause of Dorian's infatuation with Sibyl, and his musing that 'one could never pay too high a price for any sensation' reveal a man who has replaced ethics with aesthetics so completely that other people exist only as material for his intellectual amusement. His reflection that experience is merely 'the name men gave to their mistakes' and that 'the sin we had done once, and with loathing, we would do many times, and with joy' theorizes about the repetitive nature of human error just before Dorian's telegram announces his impulsive engagement -- proving the point. The sunset turning windows to 'plates of heated metal' and the sky to 'a faded rose' bathes everything in a beauty that is already dying, foreshadowing the catastrophe ahead.
